record_office_terminology.land_records: Bureau of Conveyances (statewide -- all land records for all counties are recorded centrally in Honolulu, not at the county level; two systems: Regular System and Land Court System)
record_office_terminology.probate: Circuit Court (probate division) -- five judicial circuits correspond roughly to counties; First Circuit (Honolulu/Oahu), Second Circuit (Maui County), Third Circuit (Hawaii County), Fifth Circuit (Kauai County); Kalawao has no separate court
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Hawaii Department of Health (marriage licenses issued by licensed agents statewide; civil registration from Kingdom era)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1842 (Kingdom of Hawaii began civil registration of births, deaths, and marriages -- among the earliest in the US; compliance improved under Territorial government from 1900)

burned_county_authority: Hawaii has no burned counties. Record survival across all five counties is generally excellent due to the centralized nature of Hawaiian recordkeeping (Bureau of Conveyances, Department of Health, State Archives). The August 2023 Lahaina wildfire on Maui may have affected some local holdings, but state-level records in Honolulu were not impacted.
administrative_notes: Hawaii has 5 counties, all formed in 1905 under the Territorial government. Hawaii was an independent Polynesian kingdom until 1893, when the monarchy was overthrown. The Republic of Hawaii (1894-1898) preceded US annexation in 1898. Hawaii was a US Territory from 1898-1959, becoming the 50th state on August 21, 1959. Hawaiian recordkeeping is unusually centralized: the Bureau of Conveyances in Honolulu records ALL land transactions statewide (not county-level), and the Department of Health maintains ALL vital records statewide. The Hawaii State Archives holds Kingdom, Republic, and Territorial government records. Hawaiian genealogy (mo'oku'auhau) is a deeply important cultural tradition -- chiefly genealogies were maintained orally for centuries and began to be recorded in writing by missionaries in the 1820s. The Great Mahele of 1848 divided Hawaiian lands and created the modern land tenure system, generating records essential for tracing Hawaiian families. Plantation labor immigration (Chinese from 1852, Japanese from 1868, Portuguese from 1878, Korean from 1903, Filipino from 1906) created one of the most diverse populations in the US, and plantation records are KEY genealogical sources for immigrant families. Kalawao County is unique -- it has no functioning county government and consists solely of the Kalaupapa leprosy settlement administered by the Department of Health.

core_repositories.repository: Hawaii State Archives, Iolani Palace Grounds, Honolulu
core_repositories.description: The premier repository for Hawaiian genealogical research, holding records of the Kingdom of Hawaii, Republic of Hawaii, Territory of Hawaii, and State of Hawaii. Collections include: vital records indexes, Great Mahele land records, immigration and naturalization records, Board of Health records (including Kalaupapa settlement), court records, census records, royal family papers, and government correspondence. The Archives also hold extensive photograph collections and Hawaiian-language documents. Essential for all Hawaii genealogical research regardless of county.

core_repositories.repository: Bureau of Conveyances, 1151 Punchbowl Street, Honolulu
core_repositories.description: The SOLE repository for all Hawaii land records statewide. Maintains two recording systems: the Regular System (grantor-grantee index from 1845, used for most properties) and the Land Court System (Torrens title registration from 1903, provides guaranteed title). All deeds, mortgages, leases, and land agreements for every county are recorded here. Online search available through eHawaii.gov. The Great Mahele (1848) land division records, Royal Patent grants, and kuleana awards to native tenants are accessible through the Bureau and the Hawaii State Archives.

The premier Pacific cultural institution and a critical genealogical repository. Holdings include Hawaiian genealogical manuscripts (mo'oku'auhau), chiefly genealogies recorded by missionaries and Hawaiian scholars, Hawaiian-language manuscripts, photograph collections, oral history recordings, and ethnographic materials. The Bishop Museum Archives hold materials essential for tracing Hawaiian (kanaka maoli) families, including records from the Kamehameha Schools/Bishop Estate.

core_repositories.source_name: Hawaiian Mission Children's Society Library (Mission Houses Museum)
core_repositories.repository: Hawaiian Mission Children's Society Library, Honolulu
core_repositories.description: Repository for records of the American Protestant mission to Hawaii from 1820. Holdings include missionary journals, letters, and reports; vital records compiled by missionaries (births, marriages, deaths of Hawaiian congregants); church records from mission stations across all islands; Hawaiian-language materials; and the personal papers of missionary families (Bingham, Judd, Thurston, Cooke, Castle, etc.). Essential for tracing both Hawaiian and missionary-descendant families.

core_repositories.source_name: University of Hawaii at Manoa -- Hamilton Library Hawaiian Collection
core_repositories.repository: University of Hawaii at Manoa, Hamilton Library, Honolulu
core_repositories.description: Major research library with the Hawaiian Collection (books, manuscripts, maps, photographs), the Hawaii War Records Depository (WWII records), and the Hawaiian Sugar Planters' Association records. The Hawaiian Collection includes plantation records, Hawaiian-language materials, territorial government publications, and genealogical reference works. The Pacific Collection covers broader Pacific Island genealogy and migration patterns.

familysearch_collections.description: Extensive indexed and browsable collections including Hawaii vital records (births, deaths, marriages from 1842), immigration records, census records, plantation records, church records, and land records. The LDS Church established a presence in Hawaii in 1850 and has microfilmed extensively at the Hawaii State Archives, Bureau of Conveyances, and Department of Health. Key collections include Hawaii births (1859-1932), deaths (1826-1948), marriages (1826-1929), and Chinese immigration records.

Comprehensive research guide covering Hawaii's unique genealogical landscape: Kingdom-era records, Territorial records, plantation records, immigration records, Hawaiian genealogical traditions (mo'oku'auhau), and guides for researching specific ethnic groups (Hawaiian, Japanese, Chinese, Filipino, Portuguese, Korean). County-specific pages and record availability guides.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Hawaii newspapers including English-language papers (Pacific Commercial Advertiser, Honolulu Star-Bulletin, Maui News, Hawaii Tribune-Herald, Garden Island). Legal notices, obituaries, shipping news, plantation news, and community events across all islands.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: University of Hawaii at Manoa / Office of Hawaiian Affairs
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Hawaiian-language newspapers published from 1834-1948 are an extraordinary and unique genealogical source. Over 100 Hawaiian-language newspapers published genealogies (mo'oku'auhau), obituaries, birth and marriage announcements, land claim notices, and community news. The Ho'olaupa'i project and Papakilo Database have digitized and made searchable thousands of newspaper pages. Essential for researching Hawaiian families -- these newspapers contain genealogical information found nowhere else.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Hawaii Plantation Labor Immigration Records -- Hawaii State Archives
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Hawaii's plantation labor immigration is THE defining genealogical story of the state. The Board of Immigration records at the Hawaii State Archives document the organized recruitment and importation of contract laborers: Chinese (from 1852), Japanese (from 1868), Portuguese from Madeira and Azores (from 1878), Korean (from 1903), and Filipino (from 1906). Records include passenger manifests, labor contracts, plantation assignments, and correspondence. The Hawaiian Sugar Planters' Association (HSPA) coordinated much of the recruitment. These records are essential for tracing the ancestors of Hawaii's diverse immigrant communities.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Chinese Exclusion Act Case Files -- NARA San Francisco
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Chinese Exclusion Act (1882-1943) enforcement records for Hawaii. Chinese immigration to Hawaii began in 1852 and predates Exclusion, but the Act severely affected Chinese Hawaiian families after annexation in 1898. Case files contain photographs, affidavits, family information, and testimony -- remarkably detailed genealogical sources. NARA San Francisco holds the Hawaii district files. Some are digitized on FamilySearch and Ancestry.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Honolulu Immigration Station Records and Passport Records
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Honolulu was the primary port of entry for all Hawaii-bound immigrants. Immigration station records include passenger arrival manifests, quarantine records, and entry permits. Kingdom and Republic of Hawaii passport records document both incoming immigrants and outgoing Hawaiian nationals. Naturalization records from Territorial courts (1900-1952) at Hawaii State Archives and NARA.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Native Hawaiian (Kanaka Maoli) Genealogical Records -- Bishop Museum / State Archives
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: Bishop Museum / Hawaii State Archives / Hawaiian Historical Society
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Native Hawaiian genealogy (mo'oku'auhau) is among the most meticulously maintained genealogical traditions in the world, with oral genealogies tracing chiefly lines back dozens of generations. Written records begin with missionary documentation in the 1820s. Bishop Museum holds the largest collection of Hawaiian genealogical manuscripts, including the genealogies of ruling families (Kamehameha, Kalakaua, Liliuokalani). The Great Mahele records (1848) at the State Archives document land claims by Hawaiian commoners and chiefs, providing genealogical context. Hawaiian Homes Commission records (1921+) document Native Hawaiian blood quantum and family connections for homestead eligibility.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: Hawaii State Archives / Bureau of Conveyances
native_american_and_removal_records.description: The Great Mahele of 1848 was the fundamental land division that transformed Hawaiian land tenure from a feudal system to private property. The Mahele Book records the division between King Kamehameha III and 245 chiefs. The Land Commission Awards (1846-1855) documented claims by both chiefs and commoners (kuleana awards). These records contain names, locations, testimony about land use and residency, and family relationships -- they are among the most important genealogical sources for Hawaiian families. Available at the Hawaii State Archives and partially indexed online.

military_records.source_name: Hawaii Military Records -- Pearl Harbor, WWII, Territorial Guard
military_records.repository: NARA / Hawaii War Records Depository (UH Manoa) / Hawaii State Archives
military_records.description: Hawaii's military records are extensive due to its strategic Pacific location. Records include: Pearl Harbor attack casualty lists and service records (December 7, 1941); WWII-era selective service and draft registration for Hawaii; Hawaii Territorial Guard records; 100th Infantry Battalion and 442nd Regimental Combat Team records (Japanese American units); martial law records (1941-1944); military government records; and records of military installations across the islands. The Hawaii War Records Depository at UH Manoa is a unique collection of WWII-era documents.

military_records.source_name: Veteran Discharge Records Filed with Bureau of Conveyances
military_records.description: Veterans in Hawaii could file their discharge papers (DD-214 or equivalent) with the Bureau of Conveyances. These filed discharges provide military service details and can be searched through the Bureau's grantor-grantee index. Covers all branches and all wars from the Territorial period onward.

land_and_property.source_name: Hawaii Land Records -- Bureau of Conveyances (Statewide)
land_and_property.repository: Bureau of Conveyances, 1151 Punchbowl Street, Honolulu
land_and_property.description: Unlike all other US states, Hawaii records ALL land transactions in a single statewide office. The Bureau of Conveyances maintains two systems: the Regular System (grantor-grantee index from 1845) and the Land Court System (Torrens title registration from 1903). The Regular System is used for most properties. The Land Court System provides government-guaranteed title. Online search is available through eHawaii.gov. Researchers must understand both systems to trace land ownership.

land_and_property.source_name: Great Mahele, Royal Patents, and Kuleana Awards
land_and_property.repository: Hawaii State Archives / Bureau of Conveyances
land_and_property.description: The Great Mahele (1848) divided Hawaiian lands among the king, chiefs, and government. Subsequent kuleana awards granted small parcels to native tenants. Royal Patent grants conveyed government lands. These records are foundational for Hawaiian land research and contain genealogically significant testimony about families, residency, and land use. The Indices of Awards (published volumes) and the Mahele Book are at the State Archives.

vital_records.source_name: Hawaii Vital Records -- Department of Health
vital_records.repository: Hawaii Department of Health, Vital Records Section, Honolulu
vital_records.description: Hawaii has among the oldest civil registration systems in the US, dating to 1842 under the Kingdom of Hawaii. The Department of Health holds birth, death, and marriage records from 1842 to the present for ALL counties statewide. Registration was conducted through district agents across the islands. Compliance was best in urban Honolulu and improved significantly under the Territorial government (1900+). Certified copies available by mail or in person. Historical vital records indexes are partially available through FamilySearch. Delayed birth certificates are available for births not originally registered.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Hawaii Church Records -- Protestant Mission, Catholic, Buddhist, Shinto
church_and_cemetery.repository: Hawaiian Mission Children's Society Library / Bishop Museum / Local congregations
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records in Hawaii span an extraordinary range of denominations reflecting the state's diverse population. American Protestant (Congregational) mission records from 1820; Catholic records from the 1830s; Mormon (LDS) records from 1850; Buddhist temple records for Japanese communities (Hongwanji, Jodo, Shingon, Soto Zen); Shinto shrine records; Chinese Christian mission records; Korean Christian church records; Filipino Catholic and Protestant records. Church records are essential for pre-civil-registration vital events and for documenting communities not well represented in government records.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Find A Grave / Hawaii State Archives / County governments
church_and_cemetery.description: Cemetery records for all Hawaiian islands including church cemeteries, community cemeteries, ethnic community cemeteries (Japanese, Chinese, Korean, Filipino), military cemeteries (National Memorial Cemetery of the Pacific -- Punchbowl), and family burial plots. Hawaiian burial practices include both Christian cemeteries and traditional Hawaiian burial sites (protected under state law). Burial permits from the Board of Health are at the Hawaii State Archives.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Founded in 1892, the Hawaiian Historical Society holds manuscript collections, photographs, and published historical materials. Publishes the Hawaiian Journal of History. Library holdings include published genealogies, family histories, and reference works for Hawaiian research.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: Hawaii State Public Library System -- Hawaiian Collection
research_guides_and_societies.description: The Hawaii State Library's Hawaiian Collection includes published genealogies, county histories, Hawaiian-language materials, city directories, and genealogical reference works. Branch libraries on neighbor islands hold local community resources.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: Sandwich Islands Genealogical Society / Hawaii GenWeb
research_guides_and_societies.description: Volunteer genealogical resources for Hawaii research. The Sandwich Islands Genealogical Society is the primary statewide genealogical society. Hawaii GenWeb provides county-level pages with cemetery transcriptions, query boards, and research guides.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Hawaii has no extinct counties. The five current counties -- Hawaii, Honolulu, Kalawao, Kauai, and Maui -- were all created simultaneously in 1905 under Act 39 of the Territorial Legislature. Before 1905, Hawaii had no county system; governance was conducted at the island and district level under the Kingdom (until 1893), Republic (1894-1898), and early Territorial (1898-1905) governments. The historical districts (moku) of each island -- such as Hilo, Kona, Kohala, Ka'u, Hamakua, and Puna on the Big Island -- predate Western contact and remain relevant for land records and genealogical research, but they were never 'counties' in the American sense. Kalawao County is functionally an administrative unit of the Department of Health rather than a true county government, but it has existed as a county since 1905.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Hawaii has no burned counties. Record survival is generally excellent across all five counties due to Hawaii's centralized recordkeeping system (Bureau of Conveyances for land, Department of Health for vital records, State Archives for government records). The August 2023 Lahaina wildfire on Maui caused devastating community losses but did not affect the centralized state repositories in Honolulu. Some local records held in Lahaina may have been impacted. Kingdom-era records (pre-1893) are well preserved at the Hawaii State Archives. Hurricane Iniki (1992) affected some local records on Kauai but state-level repositories were not damaged.
